description.task: Practice safe DELETE statements with WHERE conditions to remove specific rows.
instructions: Run INSERT and initial GROUP BY count.
Expected remaining: 1 WARN, 2 ERROR, 1 INFO (from Jan 3), 1 INFO (Jan 4 backup).
Add a comment: how would you archive old logs to a separate table BEFORE deleting them?
description.hint: To archive before deleting: first INSERT INTO logs_archive SELECT * FROM logs WHERE log_level = 'DEBUG'; then DELETE FROM logs WHERE log_level = 'DEBUG';
description.expectedOutput:
After deletes: ~5 rows remain (2 ERROR, 1 WARN, 2 INFO from Jan 3+)
DEBUG and old INFO rows removed

description.task: Use DELETE with a subquery to remove rows based on conditions in a related table.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name        VARCHAR(60),
  email       VARCHAR(80),
  status      VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'active'
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id INT,
  total       DECIMAL(8,2),
  order_date  DATE,
  FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id)
);

INSERT INTO customers VALUES
  (1, 'Alice', 'alice@mail.com', 'active'),
  (2, 'Bob',   'bob@mail.com',   'inactive'),
  (3, 'Carol', 'carol@mail.com', 'active'),
  (4, 'Dave',  'dave@mail.com',  'inactive'),
  (5, 'Eve',   'eve@mail.com',   'active');

INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (101, 1, 150.00, '2026-01-05'),
  (102, 3, 200.00, '2026-01-06'),
  (103, 2,  80.00, '2026-01-07'),   -- Bob (inactive)
  (104, 5, 120.00, '2026-01-08'),
  (105, 4,  50.00, '2026-01-09');   -- Dave (inactive)

-- Find orders belonging to inactive customers
SELECT o.order_id, c.name, c.status, o.total
FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE c.status = 'inactive';

-- Delete those orders using a subquery
DELETE FROM orders
WHERE customer_id IN (
  SELECT customer_id FROM customers WHERE status = 'inactive'
);

-- Verify only active customer orders remain
SELECT order_id, customer_id, total FROM orders ORDER BY order_id;
instructions: Run all CREATE TABLE and INSERT statements.
Run the pre-delete SELECT to see inactive customer orders.
Run the final SELECT — only orders 101, 102, 104 should remain.
Write another DELETE: remove all inactive customers (now safe since their orders are gone).
description.hint: DELETE with IN subquery: the subquery returns a list of customer_ids, and the outer DELETE removes all orders where customer_id matches any in that list.
description.expectedOutput:
Pre-delete: orders 103 (Bob) and 105 (Dave) identified
Post-delete: 3 orders remain (101, 102, 104)

description.task: Implement the soft-delete pattern: instead of physically deleting rows, mark them as deleted with a flag column.
description.context: In production systems, hard deletes are often replaced by soft deletes — setting is_deleted=TRUE or deleted_at=NOW(). This preserves audit history and allows recovery.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE tasks (
  task_id     INT PRIMARY KEY,
  title       VARCHAR(100),
  assignee    VARCHAR(50),
  status      VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'open',
  created_at  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  deleted_at  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NULL,  -- NULL = not deleted
  is_deleted  BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE
);

INSERT INTO tasks VALUES
  (1, 'Fix login bug',          'Alice', 'done',        NOW()-INTERVAL '10 days', NULL, FALSE),
  (2, 'Write documentation',   'Bob',   'open',        NOW()-INTERVAL '5 days',  NULL, FALSE),
  (3, 'Deploy to staging',     'Carol', 'in_progress', NOW()-INTERVAL '2 days',  NULL, FALSE),
  (4, 'Review PR #42',         'Alice', 'open',        NOW()-INTERVAL '1 day',   NULL, FALSE),
  (5, 'Old task from 2024',    'Dave',  'open',        NOW()-INTERVAL '100 days',NULL, FALSE);

-- Soft-delete task 5 (old task)
UPDATE tasks
SET is_deleted = TRUE, deleted_at = NOW()
WHERE task_id = 5;

-- Active view: exclude soft-deleted rows
SELECT task_id, title, assignee, status
FROM tasks
WHERE is_deleted = FALSE
ORDER BY created_at;

-- Admin view: all tasks including deleted
SELECT task_id, title, is_deleted, deleted_at
FROM tasks
ORDER BY task_id;

-- Restore a soft-deleted task
UPDATE tasks SET is_deleted = FALSE, deleted_at = NULL WHERE task_id = 5;

SELECT COUNT(*) AS active_tasks FROM tasks WHERE is_deleted = FALSE;
instructions: Run all INSERT and soft-delete UPDATE statements.
Run the active view — task 5 should not appear.
Run the admin view — task 5 appears with is_deleted=TRUE.
Run the restore UPDATE and verify active_tasks count goes back to 5.
Add a comment: what are two business advantages of soft delete over hard delete?
description.hint: Advantages of soft delete: (1) Audit trail — you know when and what was deleted. (2) Recovery — you can undo accidental deletions. Disadvantages: table grows over time; queries must always filter is_deleted=FALSE.
description.expectedOutput:
Active view: 4 tasks (task 5 excluded)
Admin view: 5 tasks, task 5 shows is_deleted=TRUE
After restore: active_tasks = 5
